title: Small But Funny: A Feedback-Driven Approach to Humor Distillation
research_questions: How does the teacher perform as a critic?
How do students trained with and without feedback compare?
How frequently should the teacher intervene?
How does data size affect student performance?
Does the length bias of the critic affect student responses?

datasets_used.name: EmpatheticDialogues
datasets_used.size: 12,000 sentences from the training set; 1,000 sentences from the validation and test sets; 100 samples from the test set for human evaluation

methods_proposed.name: Feedback-driven humor distillation
methods_proposed.summary: A two-phase distillation framework in which an LLM first acts as a teacher by generating humorous paraphrases for literal inputs, and then acts as a critic by providing pairwise feedback on student outputs.

methods_proposed.name: Critique-phase feedback incorporation
methods_proposed.summary: The student generates k = 6 candidates, a diverse pair is selected using maximum pairwise n-gram-based edit distance, and an LLM critic scores the pair with Multiple Choice Prompting. The feedback is incorporated using DPO, BRIO, or a combined BRIO-DPO variant.

methods_proposed.name: BART-BRIO-DPO
methods_proposed.summary: A combined feedback variant where the student is first trained with a BRIO ranking objective using teacher-ranked teacher responses, then initialized for DPO training using preference pairs from the student.

evaluation_methods.detail: Win Tie Rate (WTR) compares a student paraphrase against a teacher paraphrase using Multiple Choice Prompting; Llama2-70B and GPT-4 are used as critics.
Some of the authors performed blind pairwise scoring of 100 humorous output pairs for humor and consistency, enabling comparison of LLM judgments with human judgments.
Appendix A reports human WTR-H, WTR-C, and WTR for student models against LLAMA2-70B.
The critic is analyzed with LLM-Human Agreement for humor and consistency, Positional Bias, and Length Bias; WTR is scaled by 100 in the reported tables.

headline_results.finding: The best main-test student, BART-BRIO-DPO, reached the highest WTR against the Llama2-70B teacher under the Llama2 critic.
Under the GPT-4 critic, BART-BRIO-DPO also had the highest WTR against the teacher, ahead of BART-DPO and BART-BRIO.
On the OOD test set, feedback-trained models improved over BART-FT, with BART-BRIO-DPO achieving the best WTR.
Multiple Choice Prompting with overall WTR achieved the strongest reported human agreement for humor while retaining measurable position and length biases.
headline_results.metric: Ag-H; Ag-C; PB; LB, scores multiplied by 100

headline_results.finding: More frequent feedback improved BART-BRIO performance in the data/frequency experiment.
In human evaluation, BART-DPO had the highest student WTR against LLAMA2-70B among the reported groups.

key_findings: Feedback-based distillation substantially narrowed the gap between BART-large students and the Llama2-70B teacher on conditional humor generation.
BART-BRIO-DPO was best in the main automatic evaluation, scoring WTR_GPT4 = 56 and WTR_llama2 = 65 against the teacher, compared with BART-FT at 30 and 28.
Both BRIO and DPO feedback objectives improved over imitation-only training, and their combination further improved main-test WTR by 4-6% over DPO or BRIO independently according to the paper text.
The Llama2-70B critic using Multiple Choice Prompting matched human humor judgments with Ag-H up to 76, but showed position and length biases.
More frequent teacher feedback improved student performance in Table 4, with BART-BRIO at 12K data increasing from 43 with 1/10 epochs feedback to 66 with 10/10 epochs feedback.
Feedback was more data-efficient than simply scaling imitation data: a feedback model trained on 12K examples outperformed a no-feedback model trained on 36K examples in Table 4.
limitations: Quantifying humor is inherently subjective and may not be accurately captured using simple metrics.
The generated humorous outputs sometimes refer to celebrities and events in North America, so the models may not generalize well across demographic and cultural groups.
Training on LLM feedback can propagate biases from the LLM training data into the smaller model.
Biases in model evaluation, including length bias and positional bias, can also be propagated to the student.
Without mitigation strategies, length bias in the teacher can make student outputs almost double compared to the input text.
More frequent feedback improves performance but increases communication cost with the teacher.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper notes that the public datasets may contain offensive, biased, or hateful content. The authors used the chat variant of Llama2, which is aligned to generate safe warnings and responses when offensive content may be present; Llama2 generated such warnings for about 3-4% of inputs, and these inputs were discarded. The paper also warns that celebrity analogies, especially with negative connotations, may be considered offensive to specific people.

human_samples.population: Some of the paper authors, described as “annotators.”
human_samples.recruitment: Internal author annotation; no recruitment procedure reported.
human_samples.observations: The paper says annotators performed blind pairwise scoring of 100 pairs of humorous outputs, ranking paraphrases for humor and consistency. The dataset section also says 100 test samples were used for human evaluation. Appendix A reports human WTR-H/WTR-C/WTR for BART, BART-BRIO, and BART-DPO against LLAMA2-70B, but does not state annotator count or exact pair counts per group.
human_samples.detail: Used to validate Llama2-70B critic agreement and to provide a small human evaluation of selected student models versus the teacher.

judges.name: Llama2-70B chat
judges.detail: Used as teacher to generate humorous paraphrases; used as critic with Multiple Choice Prompting for pairwise Win Tie Rate; used to label positive/negative preference pairs for feedback training; used for main automatic WTR_llama2 evaluation against the teacher and for critic-bias/agreement analyses.

judges.name: GPT-4
judges.detail: Used as an external critic for automatic WTR_GPT4 evaluation of student outputs against the Llama2-70B teacher in Table 2. Exact GPT-4 snapshot/version not reported.

judges.name: author annotators
judges.detail: Some authors performed blind pairwise scoring of 100 output pairs for humor and consistency; their judgments were compared with Llama2-70B critic judgments and reported in Appendix human evaluation.

judges.name: maximum pairwise n-gram-based edit distance
judges.detail: During critique-phase candidate construction, k=6 student candidates were generated and a diverse pair was selected using maximum pairwise n-gram-based edit distance before LLM critic scoring.

generation_selection.scope: Teacher data generation for imitation training
generation_selection.n: N=3 humorous paraphrases per literal input, all used to create literal-humorous pairs rather than selected as a best output
generation_selection.detail: Llama2-70B was prompted in zero-shot form to generate 3 creative and humorous paraphrases with the exact same meaning as the input; 12,000 training sentences yielded 36,000 literal-humorous pairs.

generation_selection.scope: Student candidate generation for critique-phase feedback
generation_selection.detail: The finetuned student generated 6 candidates using either diverse beam search or nucleus sampling. A pair of diverse paraphrases was selected from this pool by maximum pairwise n-gram-based edit distance, then scored by the LLM critic. Selection was for diversity, not directly for humor quality.

generation_selection.scope: BRIO-DPO BRIO supervision from teacher responses
generation_selection.n: two teacher responses ranked; total generated candidates not fully specified beyond teacher responses
generation_selection.detail: For BRIO-DPO, contrastive pairs for BRIO training came from the teacher instead of the student by letting the teacher rank two of its own responses; DPO preference pairs still came from student candidates.

generation_selection.scope: Student model outputs for automatic and human evaluation
generation_selection.n: one evaluated student output per input; beam search used with number of beams set to 5
generation_selection.detail: All BART student responses used for evaluation were obtained with beam search with num_beams=5. No best-of-N selection of evaluated outputs is reported.

WTR compares fixed pairs of outputs, typically a student output versus a teacher output. Reversing candidate order for positional-bias mitigation is repeated evaluation of the same fixed outputs, not generation selection.

model_provenance.name: Llama 2
model_provenance.detail: Used as teacher and as Llama2 critic. Paper cites Touvron et al. (2023) but does not report a checkpoint hash, service, access date, or inference date.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4
model_provenance.detail: Used as external automatic critic for WTR_GPT4. No exact OpenAI model snapshot, access method, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: BART-large
model_provenance.version_or_snapshot: BART-large from Lewis et al. (2020); fine-tuned variants include BART-FT-WS, BART-FT, BART-SD, BART-BRIO, BART-DPO, and BART-BRIO-DPO
model_provenance.detail: Student SLM family. The paper reports fine-tuning objectives and epochs but not exact pretrained checkpoint identifier or random seeds.

model_provenance.name: BART-DPO-ITER2
model_provenance.version_or_snapshot: BART-large variant with two feedback iterations
model_provenance.detail: Mentioned in qualitative analysis/Table 5 as a variant receiving feedback for two iterations instead of one; full experimental settings are not reported.

inference_budgets.scope: Teacher Llama2-70B generation of imitation data
inference_budgets.detail: 12,000 EmpatheticDialogues training sentences were sampled and used to generate 36,000 literal-humorous pairs. The prompt asked for a numbered list of 3 paraphrases. Total LLM calls, max tokens, hardware, and cost are not reported.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: one evaluated response per input; beam search used internally with 5 beams
inference_budgets.detail: The paper states all BART student responses are obtained using beam search with num_beams=5 during generation. Max length and other decoding parameters are not reported.

inference_budgets.scope: Student candidate generation for feedback training
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: k = 6 candidates per input before pair selection
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: either diverse beam search or nucleus sampling; exact parameters not reported
inference_budgets.detail: A diverse candidate pair was selected using maximum pairwise n-gram-based edit distance. Candidate training pairs were filtered to near-equal length with length ratio 0.8 to 1.2 to mitigate length bias.

inference_budgets.scope: LLM critic scoring for WTR and feedback labels
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: The paper states win rates are averaged by letting paraphrases take both position 1 and position 2; total call count is not reported.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Multiple Choice Prompting; critic predicts choice token 1 or 2 with associated probabilities. Decoding/log-probability settings are not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Used for training feedback and automatic evaluation. Cloze prompting was also tested in RQ1 but MCP WTR was selected for main evaluation.

inference_budgets.scope: Training/feedback schedule relevant to inference demand
inference_budgets.detail: BART-FT-WS was fine-tuned for 10 epochs on teacher data. BART-BRIO and BART-DPO were fine-tuned for 10 epochs with feedback objectives; BART-BRIO-DPO used 10 BRIO epochs followed by 10 DPO epochs. Table 4 varies feedback frequency at 1/10, 2/10, and 10/10 epochs, but does not report total LLM call counts or cost.

human_baselines.description: No human-written humorous paraphrase baseline or human generator baseline is evaluated. Humans serve as judges/validators rather than as a directly comparable generation system.
human_baselines.detail: Model outputs are compared against the Llama2-70B teacher and judged by LLM critics and a small author-annotated human evaluation.

mitigations: Evaluation uses validation/test splits sampled separately from the 12,000 EmpatheticDialogues training inputs used for student distillation.
Humorous references and student outputs are newly generated rather than legacy gold humor labels.
Out-of-distribution evaluation uses 500 inputs from Samsum.
Inputs that triggered Llama2 safety warnings, about 3-4% of inputs, were discarded; this is a safety filtering step, not a contamination control.
contamination_risk.basis: EmpatheticDialogues and Samsum are public 2019 datasets, preceding Llama2 and GPT-4. The paper does not report decontamination checks for Llama2, GPT-4, or BART pretraining data. Because the task uses public legacy source inputs with later LLMs as teacher/critics, but the evaluated humorous outputs are newly generated rather than fixed public labels, risk is assessed as moderate rather than high.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Headline claims that feedback-trained BART students narrow the gap with or perform on par with the Llama2-70B teacher, comparisons among BART-FT/BART-SD/BART-BRIO/BART-DPO/BART-BRIO-DPO, OOD performance, data-size effects, feedback-frequency effects, and critic-bias analyses all rely heavily on LLM WTR judgments. Llama2-70B also supplies training feedback labels.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Some author annotators performed blind pairwise scoring of 100 pairs; the paper reports LLM-human agreement up to 76% for humor with MCP WTR and Appendix A reports human WTR scores for three student groups against LLAMA2-70B. Annotator count and aggregation details are not reported.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: Llama2-70B is both teacher and critic for many experiments, while GPT-4 provides an external LLM-critic comparison for Table 2. Human evaluation is limited and secondary relative to the automatic LLM-based results.

reporting_gaps: Exact number of human annotators is not reported; “some of the authors” is the only participant description.
Human annotator demographics, geography, recruitment/compensation, training, aggregation procedure, and inter-annotator agreement are not reported.
Appendix A does not specify exact number of human-evaluated pairs per group or whether all groups used the same 100 samples.
Exact GPT-4 model snapshot/version, API/access method, inference date, and decoding/log-probability settings are not reported.
Exact Llama2-70B checkpoint identifier, hosting/access method, inference date, and total inference-call count are not reported.
BART-large pretrained checkpoint identifier, random seeds, hardware, training compute, and cost are not reported.
Max input/output token limits and generation length limits are not reported for teacher, student, or critic calls.

For student candidate generation in feedback training, the paper says either diverse beam search or nucleus sampling but does not report which settings were used in each experiment or the nucleus/diverse-beam parameters.
Total LLM communication cost/call volume for feedback-frequency experiments is not reported despite the paper noting a cost trade-off.
No decontamination or training-data overlap analysis is reported for public EmpatheticDialogues/Samsum inputs with Llama2, GPT-4, or BART pretraining data.
The exact counts and handling of the 3-4% Llama2 safety-warning inputs discarded from distilled data are not fully specified.
